figures.name: Ammon (The Oracle of Siwa)
figures.gloss: The supreme, ram-horned oracle god of the Libyan desert. Unlike gods who spoke through texts or human mouthpieces, Ammon communicated his infallible prophecies through the kinetic movements of his unhewn idol, carried on the shoulders of priests in a golden boat.
figures.facet: Unlike the dynastic, state-building Amun of Egypt, this Libyan Ammon is radically isolated in a lethal desert oasis. His power relies on absolute remoteness and aniconic motion rather than monumental decrees.
figures.provenance: Herodotus (Book 2); Diodorus Siculus; Callisthenes's account of Alexander the Great's visit.

figures.name: Gurzil (The Bull of War)
figures.gloss: A fierce, bull-headed god of war venerated by the Laguatan and Austuriani tribes. The son of Ammon and a divine cow, he was physically carried into the vanguard of battle as a sacred bull or idol to ensure military victory.
figures.facet: He embodies a highly mobile, martial pastoralism. His divine presence is not locked in a static sanctuary but is weaponized and unleashed on the battlefield as a living engine of anti-imperial resistance.

figures.name: Anzar (Lord of the Rain)
figures.gloss: The personification of rain in the Amazigh cosmos. A vital, atmospheric force who descends to earth only when formally courted and wedded to a beautiful mortal bride during times of catastrophic drought.
figures.facet: His power is entirely contingent on human invitation and aesthetic perfection. He withholds rain not in moral judgment, but out of longing for the 'Tislit' (Bride), requiring courtship rather than mere submission.
figures.provenance: Kabyle and Chaoui ethnographic records; Emile Laoust's 'Mots et choses berberes'.

figures.name: Tislit n Anzar (Bride of the Rain)
figures.gloss: A highly formalized drought rite where a beautifully adorned wooden spoon, ladle, or effigy—historically a living woman—is carried in procession and symbolically offered in marriage to Anzar to coax the rains.
figures.facet: It domesticates ecological disaster into a communal courtship ritual. The cosmos is healed and balanced not through violent blood sacrifice, but through a simulated, joyful marriage ceremony that binds the sky to the earth.
figures.provenance: Jean Servier; continuous indigenous oral traditions across the Maghreb.

figures.name: Ifri (The Cave Mother)
figures.gloss: The ancient Amazigh goddess of caves, subterranean sanctuaries, and protection. Her name, rooted in the local term for 'cave', was eventually Latinized into 'Africa', lending her identity to the entire Roman province and continent.
figures.facet: Her divinity is inseparable from the geological reality of the Atlas mountains. She is not a distant celestial queen, but the physical, sheltering stone itself—a localized chthonic mother who absorbs rather than rules.
figures.provenance: Pliny the Elder; Latin epigraphy at Cirta; Roman syncretism as 'Dea Africa'.

figures.name: Antaios (Änti)
figures.gloss: The legendary earth-born titan of Tingis (Tangier) who wrestled all intruders to death. He drew limitless, regenerating strength directly from his mother, the Earth, whenever his physical body made contact with the soil.
figures.facet: He brilliantly subverts the classical hero-slayer paradigm. Here, the 'monster' is the invincible indigenous defender of the local soil, only defeated when physically severed from his ancestral land by a foreign colonizer.
figures.provenance: Pindar's 'Isthmian Odes'; Apollodorus; Pomponius Mela (locating his shield and tomb in Tingis).

figures.name: Tumulus Incubation
figures.gloss: The ancient Libyco-Berber practice of incubation, where tribesmen slept upon the massive stone tumuli (bazinas) of their ancestors to receive legally binding prophecies, medical cures, and judgments in their dreams.
figures.facet: It bypasses celestial intermediaries entirely. The dead do not depart for a distant underworld; they remain physically rooted in the landscape as the active, supreme arbiters of tribal law and truth.
figures.provenance: Herodotus (Book 4.172); Pomponius Mela; archaeological evidence of bazinas.

figures.name: Dii Mauri (The Moorish Gods)
figures.gloss: A collective pantheon of localized numinous ancestors and landscape spirits—such as Macurgum, Macurtam, and Varsissima—invoked heavily for physical protection, hunting success, and tribal sovereignty.
figures.facet: They fiercely resist individual mythologizing. They exist as an impenetrable, pluralistic collective of indigenous sovereignty that Roman colonizers could formally invoke but never truly assimilate into classical theology.
figures.provenance: Epigraphic dedications across Roman North Africa, particularly at Lambaesis and Chemtou.

edges.note: Intra-tradition: The supreme desert oracle and his weaponized, bull-headed son who enforces tribal will on the battlefield.
Intra-tradition: The atmospheric force of rain and the formalized bridal rite required to summon him to the earth.
Intra-tradition: The collective ancestral spirits and the physical mechanism of dream-incubation used to consult them.
Intra-tradition: The protective, chthonic earth-mother and the invincible earth-born giant who defends her territory.
GOLD SEAM: Explicitly syncretized at Siwa as Zeus Ammon, fusing the Greek sky-father with the Libyan ram-god.
GOLD SEAM: Worshipped across the empire as Jupiter Ammon, blending the supreme Roman sovereign with the desert oracle.
GOLD SEAM: Supreme oracle gods presiding over harsh desert environments, communicating truth through aniconic objects or divination rather than inscribed texts.
GOLD SEAM: Fierce, storm-associated war deities intimately linked to the bull and invoked to secure military victory.
GOLD SEAM: Bull-gods who embody violent, masculine power and atmospheric turbulence, striking down the enemies of their people.
GOLD SEAM: Warrior gods of nomadic and semi-nomadic tribes, represented in battle and sanctuary by unhewn stones or aniconic idols rather than human statues.
GOLD SEAM: Male forces of fertility and atmospheric renewal whose presence requires the ardent, formalized courtship of a female counterpart.
GOLD SEAM: Gods of agricultural renewal who vanish or withhold their life-giving essence, requiring intricate ritual appeasement to restore the world.
GOLD SEAM: Deeply localized, chthonic mother goddesses whose divinity is literally synonymous with the earth and sheltering stone.
GOLD SEAM: Earth goddesses strongly associated with caves and the subterranean realm, serving as both maternal protectors and primordial forces.
GOLD SEAM: Chthonic female entities associated with the protection of boundaries, the deceased, and subterranean spaces.
GOLD SEAM: Invincible, earth-born giants who are utterly impervious to physical defeat as long as they remain connected to their terrestrial base.
GOLD SEAM: Traditions where the revered dead do not depart for an ethereal afterlife, but remain physically present to actively adjudicate the affairs of the living.
GOLD SEAM: The active consultation of ancestral spirits who maintain ultimate, localized authority over the protection, health, and law of the tribe.
GOLD SEAM: Anonymous, powerful collectives of ancestral and landscape spirits that govern tribal sovereignty without needing individual anthropomorphic myths.

proposedArchetypes.name: Sacred Marriage
proposedArchetypes.gloss: A ritualized marital union between a mortal (or their effigy) and a natural force, designed to coax life-giving elements like rain or fertility through desire rather than blood sacrifice.
proposedArchetypes.reason: Required for rites like Tislit n Anzar or the Dogon Lebe, where ecological catastrophe is resolved by courting the divine rather than killing for it.

proposedArchetypes.name: Ancestral Oracle
proposedArchetypes.gloss: The practice of resting or sleeping upon the physical graves of the ancestors to receive unmediated, legally binding prophecies and judgments through dreams.
proposedArchetypes.reason: Essential for traditions where the dead are not worshipped abstractly, but consulted physically as the highest ongoing legal and medical authorities of the community.
